messages.content:
I can’t run tasks from here. I can, however, show exactly how you can run “load test task 1” yourself — and help interpret results or fix failures if you paste logs/output. Quick reasoning: I don’t have execution access, so I’ll give concrete run commands and checks you can copy/paste.

A. Quick question
- Which load-test framework / runner does this repo use for “task 1”? (k6, locust, artillery, JMeter, vegeta, a Docker Compose job, an npm script, a Makefile target, CI job name, etc.)
- Where is the test defined (path or script name) or what command do you normally run?

If you tell me those two things I’ll give exact commands for your repo. Meanwhile, here are general instructions and common examples.

B. General checklist (before running)
1. Ensure the system under test (SUT) is reachable and running (local service, staging URL, docker-compose up, etc.).
2. Install the load test tool (k6, locust, artillery, etc.) or ensure the project’s dev dependencies are installed.
3. Set required env vars (TARGET_URL, API_KEY, etc.).
4. Confirm any authentication or test data is prepared.
5. Run the test and capture output/logs.

C. Example run commands by tool

- k6 (common)
  - Install: brew install k6 / apt / download binary.
  - Run script: k6 run tests/task1.js
  - With env and options: TARGET_URL=https://example.com k6 run --vus 50 --duration 2m tests/task1.js

- locust (Python)
  - Install: pip install locust
  - Run: locust -f tests/task1.py --host=https://example.com --headless -u 200 -r 20 --run-time 5m --csv=task1-results
  - Open UI: locust -f tests/task1.py (then browse to http://localhost:8089)

- artillery (Node)
  - Install: npm install -g artillery
  - Run: artillery run tests/task1.yml
  - With target override: TARGET_URL=https://example.com artillery run --target $TARGET_URL tests/task1.yml

- JMeter
  - Run non-GUI: jmeter -n -t tests/task1.jmx -l results.jtl -Jduration=300
  - Then view with JMeter GUI or convert results to CSV/HTML.

- vegeta
  - Example: echo "GET https://example.com/path" | vegeta attack -rate=200/s -duration=60s | tee results.bin | vegeta report

- Docker / docker-compose
  - If there’s a service target in docker-compose: docker-compose up -d sut
  - Run a loadtest container: docker-compose run --rm loadtest task1 (replace with actual service/command)

- npm script / Makefile
  - npm: npm run loadtest:task1
  - make: make load-test-task1

D. What to look at in results (key metrics)
- Requests per second (throughput)
- Latency percentiles: p50, p95, p99
- Error rate and error types (timeouts, 5xx, connection refused)
- CPU / memory / network utilization on SUT
- Saturation indicators: response times increasing with load, high queue depth, GC spikes

E. Common troubleshooting tips
- If test fails immediately: verify TARGET_URL, DNS, and firewall.
- High error rate: reduce concurrency, check server logs, check DB limits/connections.
- Very high latencies: check CPU, disk I/O, DB slow queries.
- If load generator machine becomes the bottleneck: run distributed workers or use smaller VUs per instance.

F. If you want me to run it for you
- I can’t execute it here. If you want step-by-step tailored commands, tell me:
  1) the load-test tool,
  2) the test script path or CI task name,
  3) the target URL and desired concurrency/duration,
  4) whether you want a headless run or local interactive UI.

If you need code/test changes (e.g., modify the load test script), submit again with a concrete coding request or turn off Chat-only mode.
